story_title: The Search For Meaning In Philosophy

The search for meaning in philosophy starts with a simple observation:<[silence:80ms]> humans ask "Why?"—even when no answer exists.<[silence:100ms]> Like a dog circling to lie down, we repeat the motion, hoping this time it’ll feel right.<[silence:100ms]>

clean_story:
Questions without answers. Humans chasing purpose.

The search for meaning in philosophy starts with a simple observation: humans ask "Why?"—even when no answer exists. Like a dog circling to lie down, we repeat the motion, hoping this time it’ll feel right.

Philosophers split into camps. Some, like Aristotle, said meaning comes from flourishing—using your skills (a baker bakes, a runner runs) to live well. Others, like Nietzsche, called meaning a story we invent to survive chaos. Then there’s Camus: life has no built-in purpose, but rebelling against that fact is the purpose. Imagine shouting at a silent sky—your voice is the only reply, and that’s enough.

Tools for the search? Logic (Socrates’ questions), faith (Kierkegaard’s "leap"), or sheer stubbornness (Sisyphus pushing his rock). No method guarantees an answer. The hunt itself might be the point.

Three related subjects are existential dread, stoic acceptance, and why art feels meaningful.
